verses.text: The work is finished. Every creature, every star is part of what Scripture calls the host of heaven and earth — the full company of creation, complete.
God doesn't rest because He is tired. He stops because the work is complete — and to mark something sacred about the seventh day itself.
The blessing extends to a day — not a creature, but time itself. God sanctifies the seventh, the first thing in Scripture called holy. Rest is woven into creation's design.

Here the Bible shifts gears. The phrase 'these are the generations' is a structural marker — Genesis is now zooming in on humanity. Notice the name LORD God (Yahweh Elohim) appears together for the first time.
Before the earth grew lush, there was no rain yet, and no one to work the soil. Instead, a mist rose up to water the ground — quiet, hidden provision before humanity arrived.
Before the earth grew lush, there was no rain yet, and no one to work the soil. Instead, a mist rose up to water the ground — quiet, hidden provision before humanity arrived.
God forms the human from dust — the Hebrew word for ground (adamah) and human (adam) share a root. Then He breathes His own breath into the body. We are dust and divine breath together.
God doesn't just place the man somewhere; He prepares a garden first. Eden means 'delight.' Even before humans, God designed a place suited for joy.
Every tree was both beautiful and good to eat — God doesn't separate delight from sustenance. Two trees are named: the tree of life, and the tree of knowing good from evil. A choice is being set up.
From Eden a single river flowed out and divided into four branches. The garden is not just imagery — Genesis is locating it in real geography.
The first river, Pison, flowed through Havilah, a land rich in gold, bdellium, and onyx. Eden is described with the texture of an actual place.
The first river, Pison, flowed through Havilah, a land rich in gold, bdellium, and onyx. Eden is described with the texture of an actual place.
The second river, Gihon, encircled the land of Cush. Many scholars place this in Africa.
The last two rivers, Hiddekel (the Tigris) and Euphrates, anchor Eden firmly in the ancient Near East — the region we now call Iraq.
Work began before sin entered the world. God places the man in the garden to cultivate and protect it — labor is part of God's original design, not a punishment.
God's first instruction is generous: every tree is yours to eat from freely. The boundary comes only after this lavish gift.

One tree is off-limits — the one promising to make you the judge of good and evil. The penalty is severe: death, certain and immediate. Trusting God includes accepting that He alone defines what is good.

For the first time, God calls something not good: loneliness. The Hebrew phrase translated 'help meet' is strong — the same word is used elsewhere of God Himself helping us. This is no junior partner.
God brings every creature to the man for naming — the first act of human authority delegated by God. Naming is no small thing in Scripture.
The man names every animal, but among them no companion is found. Even surrounded by creation, Adam is alone. God knows this and prepares something more.
God puts the man into a deep sleep — the same kind of sleep that elsewhere accompanies God's prophetic acts. Adam doesn't observe his own provision. God is the only one at work.
The Hebrew word here for made means building — with care and structure. God brings the woman to the man and presents her, deliberately and formally.
Adam responds in poetry — the first human song in Scripture. The Hebrew wordplay is built in: man (ish) and woman (isha) sound related, just as their substance is one.
This single sentence becomes Scripture's foundation for marriage — a leaving of original family and a binding to another. Jesus later quotes this line when teaching about the dignity of marriage.
Naked and unashamed — before sin, there was no need to hide anything from God or from each other. This is the world as God made it. Chapter 3 will tell us how it changed.
